text: Code

O que vais precisar (tudo gratuito)

Conta no GitHub — cria uma se não tiveres
Quarto instalado no teu computador
Git instalado no teu computador

Passo 1 — Instalar o Quarto
Vai a https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/ e faz download para o teu sistema operativo.
Verifica que está instalado:
quarto --version

section: Passo 2 — Criar um repositório no GitHub
text:
Passo 2 — Criar um repositório no GitHub

Vai a github.com e faz login
Clica em “New repository”
Nome do repositório: yourusername.github.io
⚠️ Substitui yourusername pelo teu username exato do GitHub
Deixa Public
Clica “Create repository”

section: Passo 3 — Colocar os ficheiros no repositório
text:
Passo 3 — Colocar os ficheiros no repositório
Abre o terminal na pasta do site (a pasta mysite que o Claude criou) e corre:
git init
git add .
git commit -m "first commit"
git branch -M main
git remote add origin https://github.com/yourusername/yourusername.github.io.git
git push -u origin main

Passo 4 — Publicar o site
quarto publish gh-pages
O Quarto vai perguntar se queres publicar no GitHub Pages. Diz que sim.
Aguarda 1-2 minutos e o teu site está em:

https://yourusername.github.io

Como publicar novos posts

Cria uma pasta nova em posts/nome-do-post/
Cria um ficheiro index.qmd lá dentro (copia a estrutura dos posts de exemplo)
Escreve o post
Corre no terminal:

quarto publish gh-pages
Só isso. O site atualiza automaticamente.

Personalizar o site
Edita o ficheiro _quarto.yml: - Muda o título do site - Adiciona os teus links do GitHub e LinkedIn
Edita o ficheiro about.qmd: - Escreve sobre ti - Adiciona uma foto (coloca um ficheiro profile.jpg na pasta raiz)

Estrutura de pastas
mysite/
├── _quarto.yml          ← configuração do site
├── index.qmd            ← homepage
├── about.qmd            ← página sobre ti
├── posts.qmd            ← lista de todos os posts
├── styles.css           ← design personalizado
└── posts/
    ├── what-is-counterfactual/
    │   └── index.qmd    ← post teórico (já criado)
    └── causalimpact-practical/
        └── index.qmd    ← post prático (já criado)

Dúvidas?
Pede ajuda ao Claude a qualquer momento — para criar novos posts, ajustar o design, ou resolver erros.

title: What Is a Counterfactual, Really?
text:
Imagine your company runs a marketing campaign in March. Sales go up 20% that month.
Did the campaign work?
Most analysts would say yes. But that’s the wrong question — or rather, it’s an incomplete one. The right question is:

What would sales have been in March if we had never run the campaign?

That hypothetical — the world where the campaign didn’t happen — is a counterfactual. And answering it is the entire project of causal inference.

title: What Is a Counterfactual, Really?
text:
Imagine your company runs a marketing campaign in March. Sales go up 20% that month.
Did the campaign work?
Most analysts would say yes. But that’s the wrong question — or rather, it’s an incomplete one. The right question is:

What would sales have been in March if we had never run the campaign?

That hypothetical — the world where the campaign didn’t happen — is a counterfactual. And answering it is the entire project of causal inference.

title: What Is a Counterfactual, Really?
text:
The Fundamental Problem
Here’s the uncomfortable truth: we can never directly observe a counterfactual.
Every unit — a person, a company, a country — can only be in one state at a time. Either they received the treatment, or they didn’t. We observe one outcome. The other is forever hidden.
This is known as the Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference (Holland, 1986).
Formally, for unit i:
\tau_i = Y_i(1) - Y_i(0)
Where:

Y_i(1) = outcome if treated
Y_i(0) = outcome if not treated
\tau_i = individual treatment effect

We observe either Y_i(1) or Y_i(0), never both. The individual causal effect \tau_i is fundamentally unobservable.

title: What Is a Counterfactual, Really?
text:
So what do we do?
Since we can’t observe both potential outcomes for the same unit, we do the next best thing: we construct a plausible counterfactual from other data.
This can be done in several ways:






NoteThe main identification strategies










Strategy
Core idea




Randomized experiment
Randomly assign treatment, so groups are identical on average


Difference-in-differences
Use a control group that follows parallel trends


Synthetic control
Build an artificial control unit from a weighted combination of others


Regression discontinuity
Compare units just above/below an arbitrary threshold


Instrumental variables
Find a variable that affects treatment but not outcomes directly





Each strategy makes a different assumption about how to construct the counterfactual. The quality of your causal claim is only as good as the plausibility of that assumption.

title: What Is a Counterfactual, Really?
section: Why this matters more than prediction
text:
Why this matters more than prediction
Prediction and causal inference are often conflated, but they answer fundamentally different questions.
Prediction asks: given what I know, what will happen?
Causal inference asks: if I do X, what will happen because of X?
A model can be excellent at prediction and completely useless for decision-making.
Classic example: umbrella sales predict rain very well. But buying umbrellas doesn’t cause rain. If you’re a city planner deciding whether to build drainage infrastructure, “umbrella sales are high” is useful prediction but worthless causal knowledge.

title: What Is a Counterfactual, Really?
text:
Coming up
In the next posts, I’ll walk through how each identification strategy actually constructs the counterfactual — starting with difference-in-differences, which is probably the most widely used in applied work.

title: What Is a Counterfactual, Really?
text:
References

Holland, P.W. (1986). Statistics and Causal Inference. Journal of the American Statistical Association.
Pearl, J., Glymour, M., & Jewell, N.P. (2016). Causal Inference in Statistics: A Primer. Wiley.
Cunningham, S. (2021). Causal Inference: The Mixtape. Yale University Press. (Free online)

title: About
text:
I’m a data scientist focused on causal inference and forecasting — two disciplines that, together, let you not just predict the future, but understand why things happen and what would change if you acted differently.
This blog is where I share what I’m learning, building, and thinking about. Some posts are purely theoretical — working through the math and intuition behind methods like difference-in-differences or synthetic control. Others are fully practical — walking through real code and real data. Most are somewhere in between.

Topics I write about

Counterfactual analysis — synthetic controls, DiD, interrupted time series
Causal inference — DAGs, identification strategies, potential outcomes
Forecasting — probabilistic forecasting, calibration, forecast evaluation
Applied examples — using public datasets to answer real questions



Background
[Write a few lines about yourself here — your background, what you’ve worked on, what you’re looking for.]

All views are my own. Code is available on GitHub.

title: Measuring Campaign Impact with CausalImpact
text:
You run an email campaign on week 10. Sales data exists for 20 weeks. Did the campaign cause an increase in sales?
This is exactly the problem CausalImpact (Google, 2014) was built for. It fits a Bayesian structural time series model on the pre-intervention period, then extrapolates what would have happened without the intervention — your counterfactual.

title: Measuring Campaign Impact with CausalImpact
text:
You run an email campaign on week 10. Sales data exists for 20 weeks. Did the campaign cause an increase in sales?
This is exactly the problem CausalImpact (Google, 2014) was built for. It fits a Bayesian structural time series model on the pre-intervention period, then extrapolates what would have happened without the intervention — your counterfactual.

title: Measuring Campaign Impact with CausalImpact
text:
The data
library(CausalImpact)
library(tidyverse)

set.seed(42)

# Simulate 20 weeks of sales
# Campaign starts on week 10
n <- 20
time <- 1:n

# Control series (unaffected by campaign)
control <- 100 + cumsum(rnorm(n, 0, 2))

# Treatment series: follows control, then bumps up after week 10
treatment <- control + rnorm(n, 0, 3)
treatment[10:n] <- treatment[10:n] + 15  # true effect = +15

df <- data.frame(week = time, sales = treatment, control = control)

title: Measuring Campaign Impact with CausalImpact
text:
Running CausalImpact
The package needs: 1. A time series matrix (treatment + optional control series) 2. The pre/post period indices
# Define periods
pre_period  <- c(1, 9)
post_period <- c(10, 20)

# Format for CausalImpact (zoo object)
ts_data <- zoo(
  cbind(sales = df$sales, control = df$control),
  order.by = df$week
)

# Run the model
impact <- CausalImpact(ts_data, pre_period, post_period)

title: Measuring Campaign Impact with CausalImpact
text:
Reading the results
plot(impact)
summary(impact)
summary(impact, "report")
The three panels show:

Original — actual vs. predicted (counterfactual) series
Pointwise — difference at each time point
Cumulative — total accumulated effect

title: Measuring Campaign Impact with CausalImpact
text:
What the numbers mean



Metric
Meaning




Average
Estimated effect per time point


Cumulative
Total effect over the post period


p-value
Probability of this effect under no intervention



In our simulation, we injected a true effect of +15 units. CausalImpact should recover something close to that.

title: Measuring Campaign Impact with CausalImpact
text:
Key assumptions






WarningWhen this breaks down




The control series must be unaffected by the treatment.
The relationship between treatment and control must be stable before the intervention.
No other events happen at the same time.

title: Measuring Campaign Impact with CausalImpact
text:
When to use this
CausalImpact is a good fit when:

You have high-frequency data (daily/weekly)
You have no control group but have related series
The intervention has a clear start date

Classic applications: product launches, PR events, policy changes, algorithm updates.

title: Measuring Campaign Impact with CausalImpact
text:
References

Brodersen et al. (2015). Inferring causal impact using Bayesian structural time-series models. Annals of Applied Statistics.
CausalImpact R package
